EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and the Forsyth County Newspaper reported that thunderstorm winds caused considerable damage in Coal Mountain.  The tin roof of the Coal Mountain Drywall Supply company was blown off and another adjacent building under construction at the same company was blown down.  The building debris also caused damage to a nearby parked 1994 Acura Legend.   In addition, a Japanese ornamental tree was blown away on the property next door.  Other trees were reported down on Hendrix and Gravitt roads and a tree was blown down on a telephone line in Cumming near the North Forsyth High School.
The public reported one tree down in the north part of the county and another tree down in the south part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

Five Delta Air Lines employees, working on a McDonnel Douglas MD-88 aircraft parked at gate B34 at Atlanta's Hartsfield International Airport, were injured when lightning struck the tail of the aircraft.  All five employees were treated and released with only minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal. An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

A very large Canadian high pressure system settled over the southeastern United States following the cold frontal passage of the 6'th.  This high pressure system brought unseasonably cold air to the state.  Morning low temperatures from the 8'th through the 13'th were 20 to 25 degrees below normal, while high temperatures were 10 to 15 degrees below normal through the period.  Frost was widespread across north and central Georgia, while freezing temperatures were observed in a number of cities.  A number of locations in the northeast Georgia mountains recorded lows in the middle to upper 20s.  Temperatures near 32 were common across north and central Georgia during this period.  Many places reported their first freeze of the fall season a month or more earlier than the seasonal average.

The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Management Director for Stewart county reported trees down in Lumpkin which also brought down some power lines.  In addition, a funnel cloud was sighted in the area.

The Madison county 911 center reported a public sighting of a funnel cloud northwest of Comer near the intersection of Georgia Highway 98 and Georgia Highway 172.  This report was confirmed by the Deputy Sheriff.
The Laurens county 911 center reported several trees were blown down.
The Crisp county 911 center reported that two trees were blown down on Presley Road.  Another tree was blown down on a house on Canary street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia. Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition.  University of Georgia cumulative crop damage estimates for the whole state were placed at $689 million dollars, plus another $50 million dollars in increased irrigation costs, for a total estimate of $739 million in losses statewide.  Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

Extremely dry conditions continued across north and central Georgia through the month of June. These same dry conditions had persisted for most of the last 2 years. All rainfall was from spotty convective activity, with no widespread general rains occurring during the month.  Most of the convection was concentrated across the southeast parts of WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area.  The west central and north central portions received only 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall with northwest and east central portions faring slightly better with 50 to 75 percent of normal.  At the major airports in north and central Georgia, Atlanta reported 1.11 inches, Athens 1.98 inches, Macon 2.86 inches, and Columbus only 0.51 inches of rain for the month of June.  These amounts were 2.45 inches, 1.95 inches, 0.72 inches, and 3.56 inches below normal, respectively.  Yearly rainfall totals for most cooperative observer stations in north and central Georgia were between 10 and 15 inches below normal. The 2 year deficit exceeded 20 inches across much of the same area.  The center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of  central Georgia in an exceptional drought and most of north Georgia in an extreme drought state.  The northern most counties were upgraded from a first stage  drought to a severe drought status.  Water supplies continued to dwindle in most areas.  Streamflows  were at or below the lowest 10th percentile of the historical distribution for June at 90 percent of Georgia's observing sites.  Twenty-nine percent of Georgia's cotton crop was rated in a poor to very poor condition. Of that total, over $309 million was estimated for the counties in the Peachtree City CWA. Of those counties, Sumter county suffered the most with over $20 million,  and Dooly county had almost $16.5 million in losses.

The Lumber City police reported golfball size hail.
The Cherokee county 911 center reported several trees down on Georgia Highway 20 and Georgia Highway 369.
The Forsyth County 911 center reported a tree down on Pilgrim Mill Road and another tree down on Pilgrim Road on the northeast side of Cumming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

After a couple of months of relief from the prolonged dry conditions of the past 2 to 3 years, very dry conditions returned to north and central Georgia during October.  A strong cold front moved through the state on the 6'th, preceded by a line of thunderstorms.  For most locations, this represented the only rain day during the month.  Following the cold front, an unseasonably large and cold dome of high pressure settled over the state.  A stagnant pattern in the upper-levels of the atmosphere set up thereafter which allowed the strong surface high to remain over the area for an extended period of time, essentially blocking off any significant Gulf moisture for the remainder of the month.  By the end of the month, many locations were nearing records for the longest consecutive number of days without measurable rainfall.Measurable rain fell on only one day at many locations throughout the region, with monthly rainfall less than one inch at most reporting stations.  Rainfall deficits exceeding 3 inches were common across most of north Georgia, with rainfall deficits around 2 inches common in the central portion of the state.   Of the four major reporting stations in north Georgia, monthly rainfall totals of only 0.23 inch were observed at Athens,  0.87 at Atlanta, 0.62 at Columbus, and 1.08 at Macon, with deficits of 3.05 inches, 2.18 inches, 1.60 inches, and 1.10 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail.  Reported by the Mitchell County Sheriff.
Numerous trees and power lines down with scattered power outages.  Reported by Lowndes County 911.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry conditions, that had persisted for most of a 2 year period, continued through May over north and central Georgia. Rainfall amounts in central Georgia were only about 25 percent of normal, while 50 to 75 percent of normal amounts fell across the north. In Macon, the airport recorded only .30 inch of rain for the entire month, establishing this May as the driest May on record.  The previous driest May was in 1936 when only .32 inch of rain fell.  In Columbus, only .78 inch of rain was recorded for the month. Across north Georgia, rainfall amounts were around 2 inches, which was still between 2.5 and 3 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits for the year through May for most of north and central Georgia were between 7 and 10 inches. The Center for Climate Prediction and the U.S. Department of Agriculture classified most of central Georgia as being in an extreme drought for May. They classified most of the north as being in a severe drought, except for the northernmost counties which were placed in first stage drought conditions. In Georgia, corn and soybean crops were rated 43 percent poor to very poor, while cotton was rated at 37 percent poor to very poor. Dollar amounts were not available at this stage of the drought, but crops across the state would be in serious trouble without some relief from the dry conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marietta Daily Journal reported that lightning started a fire in an empty house that had just been built and was for sale.  The house was unoccupied at the time of the incident.  A firefighter was injured in the process of extinguishing the fire.

The Marietta Daily Journal reported street flooding on several streets in Marietta and Smyrna.  Allgood Road in Marietta was closed when a nearby creek flooded the road with 3 to 4 feet of water.  Cole Street was also closed as it was flooded by 3 feet of water.  In Smyrna, flooding occurred on Windy Hill Road and Reed Road, with a partial closure required on Windy Hill Road.

A stagnant pattern in the upper-levels of the atmosphere set up thereafter which allowed the strong surface high to remain over the area for an extended period of time, essentially blocking off any significant Gulf moisture for the remainder of the month.  By the end of the month, many locations were nearing records for the longest consecutive number of days without measurable rainfall.Measurable rain fell on only one day at many locations throughout the region, with monthly rainfall less than one inch at most reporting stations.  Rainfall deficits exceeding 3 inches were common across most of north Georgia, with rainfall deficits around 2 inches common in the central portion of the state.   Of the four major reporting stations in north Georgia, monthly rainfall totals of only 0.23 inch were observed at Athens,  0.87 at Atlanta, 0.62 at Columbus, and 1.08 at Macon, with deficits of 3.05 inches, 2.18 inches, 1.60 inches, and 1.10 inches respectively.

A very large Canadian high pressure system settled over the southeastern United States following the cold frontal passage of the 6'th.  This high pressure system brought unseasonably cold air to the state.  Morning low temperatures from the 8'th through the 13'th were 20 to 25 degrees below normal, while high temperatures were 10 to 15 degrees below normal through the period.  Frost was widespread across north and central Georgia, while freezing temperatures were observed in a number of cities.  A number of locations in the northeast Georgia mountains recorded lows in the middle to upper 20s.  Temperatures near 32 were common across north and central Georgia during this period.  Many places reported their first freeze of the fall season a month or more earlier than the seasonal average.

An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

Moderate drought conditions persisted  throughout the  month of September. Even as the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon moved near the area and remnants of Helene moved across much of the area, rainfall was not enough to significantly affect drought conditions. Severe drought conditions had begun the month over several immediate coastal counties of southeast Georgia,  but the small amounts of rain only improved drought conditions from severe to moderate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal and Constitution, along with the Gwinnett Daily Post, reported that lightning struck 19 homes, 2 apartments, an office park, and sparked several grass fires across the county.  The office park, located in Lilburn on Beaver Ruin Road, sustained minor damage when the attic was set on fire.  Of the 19 homes struck, significant damage was done to one on Cold Springs Drive in Lilburn.  The entire attic of the home was engulfed in flames when the firefighters arrived.  There were also two lightning related injuries in the county.  A 41-year old female was injured while pumping gas at a Texaco station on Five Forks Trickum Road.  She was barely able to move her arm for some time thereafter.  A 14-year old girl was injured when lightning struck the metal rail on which she was leaning. Immediately, her hands and feet tingled and her head hurt.  However, neither of the injuries was reported to be serious as the lightning strikes appeared to be secondary.  The fire department reported that they received 97 lightning strike related calls during the 3 1/2 hours of thunderstorm activity.
The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported that lightning started a fire on Newbridge Trace in the northern part of the Brookhaven community.
The Haralson county 911 center reported that water was flowing across several roads in the city, but no road closures were reported.

The Cherokee Tribune reported that lightning struck a home on Canton Street.  The lightning was believed to have started a fire in the kitchen.  The resulting fire completely destroyed the entire home and all of its contents.  All of the residents escaped injury.

Following the cold front, an unseasonably large and cold dome of high pressure settled over the state.  A stagnant pattern in the upper-levels of the atmosphere set up thereafter which allowed the strong surface high to remain over the area for an extended period of time, essentially blocking off any significant Gulf moisture for the remainder of the month.  By the end of the month, many locations were nearing records for the longest consecutive number of days without measurable rainfall.Measurable rain fell on only one day at many locations throughout the region, with monthly rainfall less than one inch at most reporting stations.  Rainfall deficits exceeding 3 inches were common across most of north Georgia, with rainfall deficits around 2 inches common in the central portion of the state.   Of the four major reporting stations in north Georgia, monthly rainfall totals of only 0.23 inch were observed at Athens,  0.87 at Atlanta, 0.62 at Columbus, and 1.08 at Macon, with deficits of 3.05 inches, 2.18 inches, 1.60 inches, and 1.10 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An F1 tornado touched down severely damaging a home near U.S. Highway 319, then raced northeast across Highway 32 destroying a home on Tomberlin Lane between Ocilla and Mystic.  The tornado damaged several peanut and cotton farms before dissipating.  Numerous large trees were uprooted and power lines were toppled.  Reported by the Irwin County EMA.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported that 3 to 4 inches of rain in a 2 to 3 hour period resulted in significant flooding.  Forty-eight families had to be evacuated from an apartment complex on Memorial Drive in Decatur when water from Sugar Creek spilled over its banks and into the apartments.  Flash flooding was also reported in Decatur.  Road closures were required on Commerce Drive at Clairmont Road and College Avenue at Candler Road.  On Electric Avenue, an unoccupied car was found with flood waters clear up to the roof.  At Agnes Scott College in Decatur, East College Avenue was flooded as well.
The White county 911 center, along with a former National Weather Service employee living in the area, reported several trees down, with others snapped off above ground level

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued during July over most of  WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area. Rainfall amounts were higher in July than in June. However climatological normals also increased during July, so there was still a substantial shortfall from the monthly normal precipitation. Total rainfall for the month was generally in the 2 to 3 inch range, however spotty areas received between 4 and 6 inches, while other areas received an inch or less for the month. The net effect was too little, too late for farmers across North and Central Georgia. As of the end of July, the U.S. Department of Agriculture placed all of the CWA in the range of severe to exceptional drought. The area with the largest departure from normal precipitation during July was a swath from west central Georgia across the state, generally along and south of a line from Atlanta to Athens, and north of a line from Columbus to Macon to Augusta.  No new crop damage or loss estimates were available, but the previous estimates in June seem to represent the entire 2000 growing season. That estimate placed the total for Peachtree City's county warning area at $306.7 million. An increase in precipitation toward the latter part of the month, and forecasts for the continued weakening of La Nina. brought hope that drought conditions that had lasted for over 2 years were beginning to ease. The latest 90 day forecasts called for near normal precipitation over the southeastern U.S.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golf ball size hail.
The public reported three trees down.

Several sources, including the Fulton county 911 center, a television station reporter, the Atlanta Journal and Constitution, and the public, reported trees down in various places across North Atlanta.  Trees were reported down on Johnson's Ferry Road and on several downtown Atlanta streets, including Ponce De Leon Street, Midtown Road at Techwood, and Williams Street at the Forum, all of which blocked traffic for several hours.  In addition, a fence was blown down at Chastain Park, a few miles north of downtown Atlanta.
The Fulton county 911 center reported trees down on power lines.
The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported street flooding in downtown Atlanta at the intersection of 14th Street and Northside Drive.
The Baldwin county 911 center reported high water on some roads around the county, but no road closures.

The public, along with several local newspapers, reported numerous trees and power lines down across much of the northern half of Hall county from Murrayville, to Gainesville, to Lula.  Several boats docked at the north end of Lake Lanier were also reported to have been turned over.  Damage was concentrated in the area north of Little River Bridge on U.S. 129 and south of Clermont.  The North Hall neighborhood of Muscadine Valley and River North were particularly hard hit where dozens of pine trees were snapped in half and power lines were downed. This damage was in addition to two separate F0-F1 tornadoes that touched down in Murrayville and Lula during the same time frame.

An increase in precipitation toward the latter part of the month, and forecasts for the continued weakening of La Nina. brought hope that drought conditions that had lasted for over 2 years were beginning to ease. The latest 90 day forecasts called for near normal precipitation over the southeastern U.S.

Drought conditions continued during July over most of  WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area. Rainfall amounts were higher in July than in June. However climatological normals also increased during July, so there was still a substantial shortfall from the monthly normal precipitation. Total rainfall for the month was generally in the 2 to 3 inch range, however spotty areas received between 4 and 6 inches, while other areas received an inch or less for the month. The net effect was too little, too late for farmers across North and Central Georgia. As of the end of July, the U.S. Department of Agriculture placed all of the CWA in the range of severe to exceptional drought. The area with the largest departure from normal precipitation during July was a swath from west central Georgia across the state, generally along and south of a line from Atlanta to Athens, and north of a line from Columbus to Macon to Augusta.  No new crop damage or loss estimates were available, but the previous estimates in June seem to represent the entire 2000 growing season. That estimate placed the total for Peachtree City's county warning area at $306.7 million. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Canton Cherokee Tribune and the public reported numerous trees down across mainly the western half of Cobb county from Acworth, to Kennesaw, to Powder Springs, and westward to the Paulding county line.  Most of the damage was sustained in the Apache Woods subdivision of Acworth where a 1 foot diameter tree fell through a roof causing significant damage to the structure.  Numerous other trees were reported down in this subdivision as well. Another subdivision hard hit was the English Oaks subdivision of            Kennesaw where numerous trees were downed by the thunderstorm winds.

The Douglasville County Sentinel and the public reported numerous trees down countywide. The county Emergency Management Association estimated that 75 to 100 trees were damaged in the county.  A house was significantly damaged in Winston when a tree fell through the roof. There were many reports of trees down on the roofs of homes and on power lines throughout the county.
The public reported that strong thunderstorms winds blew the guttering off a house in College Park.
The public and an amateur radio operator reported dime to golfball size hail.
The LaGrange Daily News reported trees and power lines down countywide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County News reported that lightning started a fire in the attic of a home located on Briarwood Road near Carl-Cedar Hill Road in the Briarwood Subdivision.  Fire damage was mainly confined to the attic and a front room of the house, while smoke and water damage was noted elsewhere.  No one was injured as the house was vacant at the time of the incident.
A ham radio operator relayed a report of dime size hail.
The Athens Daily News reported two trees down.
The public reported hail ranging in size from pea to golfball.

An NWS Storm Survey team from the Peachtree City WFO confirmed that an F0 to weak F1 briefly tornado touched down in the Leisure Lakes Village mobile home park area of Murrayville in the far north part of the county. At least half a dozen homes were severely damaged by falling trees. There was also substantial damage reported to other trees and power lines in the same general area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dahlonega News Nugget reported numerous trees down along Lumpkin County Parkway behind the 400 Industrial Park.  In addition, one home and an air stream trailer were damaged by falling trees.  The public reported a possible tornado in Dahlonega which uprooted trees and blew off the roofs of some homes.  While this was the same storm that shortly thereafter produced an F0 to F1 tornado in Hall county, the National Weather Service Survey Team did not see evidence of a tornado touchdown in Lumpkin county. Thus, all damage was believed to have been caused by strong straight-line thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rome News Tribune reported that thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees, one on a power line and another on a lady's car at Millican's Grocery Store in Lindale.  No injuries were reported.
The Rome News Tribune reported minor street flooding.
The public reported some trees down.
The public reported quarter size hail.
The Dade county 911 center reported some trees down in the road and on a power line.
The Walker county 911 center reported some trees down on Lytle Road.

The public and the Gainesville Times Newspaper both provided numerous reports of hail ranging in size from dimes to golfballs across the northern part of the county from Murrayville, to Gainesville, to Clermont, to Lula.  Golfball size hail was particularly widespread across the northern part of Gainesville where two reports indicated that hail was piled up 2 1/2 feet deep on roads and in yards.  One report of such hail accumulations was on Jim Hood Road north of Gainesville and another on U.S. Highway 129, also north of Gainesville.

The net effect was too little, too late for farmers across North and Central Georgia. As of the end of July, the U.S. Department of Agriculture placed all of the CWA in the range of severe to exceptional drought. The area with the largest departure from normal precipitation during July was a swath from west central Georgia across the state, generally along and south of a line from Atlanta to Athens, and north of a line from Columbus to Macon to Augusta.  No new crop damage or loss estimates were available, but the previous estimates in June seem to represent the entire 2000 growing season. That estimate placed the total for Peachtree City's county warning area at $306.7 million. An increase in precipitation toward the latter part of the month, and forecasts for the continued weakening of La Nina. brought hope that drought conditions that had lasted for over 2 years were beginning to ease. The latest 90 day forecasts called for near normal precipitation over the southeastern U.S.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs county Emergency Management Director reported that about 30 pine trees were either twisted or broken off and some power lines were down between Bullard and Danville off Interstate 16 near U.S. Highway 129.  He estimated that the damage covered a 0.5 to 1.5 mile wide area and stretched along a three mile wide path.  He further reported that while most of the trees were lying in a northwesterly direction, some of the trees were lying in a southerly direction.

Numerous reports of  estimated winds of 50 to 60 mph or greater, along with many downed trees were reported by the public from Tucker, to Stone Mountain, to Lithonia.  The majority of the downed trees occurred in the Stone Mountain area, where the Atlanta Journal and Constitution also reported that a shed was picked up and tossed about by the strong winds. Some of the trees came down on homes, causing mostly minor damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Survey Team concluded that an F0 tornado briefly touched down 5 miles west-southwest of Homer and traveled for 2 miles with an approximate path width of 100 yards to a point approximately 3 miles southwest of Homer.  One person was injured, apparently by debris, but little additional information was known. The Banks county newspaper reported that the tornado completely destroyed a chicken breeder/pullet building on Simms Road, which had 11,000 chickens inside. The number of chickens that survived was unknown, but the loss of the chicken house and chickens was estimated at $66,000.  At the neighboring residence, a satellite dish was destroyed when a large tree fell on it.  Another house on Wellborn Road sustained damage when a large uprooted tree fell on the back portion of the house causing damage. Roads and yards in the area were littered with fallen trees and debris.  Several roads were blocked as a consequence.
Large trees were blown down.
Large trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

Drought conditions continued during July over most of  WFO Peachtree City's County Warning Area. Rainfall amounts were higher in July than in June. However climatological normals also increased during July, so there was still a substantial shortfall from the monthly normal precipitation. Total rainfall for the month was generally in the 2 to 3 inch range, however spotty areas received between 4 and 6 inches, while other areas received an inch or less for the month. The net effect was too little, too late for farmers across North and Central Georgia. As of the end of July, the U.S. Department of Agriculture placed all of the CWA in the range of severe to exceptional drought. The area with the largest departure from normal precipitation during July was a swath from west central Georgia across the state, generally along and south of a line from Atlanta to Athens, and north of a line from Columbus to Macon to Augusta.  No new crop damage or loss estimates were available, but the previous estimates in June seem to represent the entire 2000 growing season. That estimate placed the total for Peachtree City's county warning area at $306.7 million. An increase in precipitation toward the latter part of the month, and forecasts for the continued weakening of La Nina. brought hope that drought conditions that had lasted for over 2 years were beginning to ease. The latest 90 day forecasts called for near normal precipitation over the southeastern U.S.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup county fire deparment, located on Hamilton Road in La Grange, reported dime size hail to the La Grange Daily Newspaper.
The public reported dime size hail.
The Monroe county 911 center reported trees down at High Falls and Plant Shear, a Georgia Power generating facility near Lake Juliette.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard county 911 center reported power lines down.
The public reported dime size hail in Woodstock and dime to quarter size hail in Holly Springs.
The public reported street flooding .

An Atlanta television station meteorologist, as well as the Atlanta Journal and Constitution, reported that a 19-year old male was injured by lightning while talking on the telephone.  The surge of electricity burned the teenager's hands and feet, but the injuries were not reported to be serious.
The Helena police reported some small trees down.  Pea size hail was also reported.
The Telfair county 911 center reported quarter size hail.
The Troup county 911 center reported trees down.
The Madisonian Newspaper reported that 15 dairy cows were killed by lightning at the Strange dairy  farm near Buckhead Road.

The Harris County Journal reported that two lightning strikes, both within a 30 minute time frame, caused considerable damage to the computer modems and the Motorola 911 system used for 911 center dispatching.  An exact damage estimate was not available.
The Peach county 911 center reported quarter size hail.

A severe thunderstorm knocked trees down in several parts of the county. A trained spotter reported several trees down across highway 26 near Elko. He also reported 2 trees down and a building hit by lightning near Perry. In addition Houston county 911 reported trees down in Kathleen, and the Warner Robins Daily Sun Newspaper reported numerous trees down on Mount Zion Road off Georgia Highway 96.
The Dooly county 911 center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of months of relief from the prolonged dry conditions of the past 2 to 3 years, very dry conditions returned to north and central Georgia during October.  A strong cold front moved through the state on the 6'th, preceded by a line of thunderstorms.  For most locations, this represented the only rain day during the month.  Following the cold front, an unseasonably large and cold dome of high pressure settled over the state.  A stagnant pattern in the upper-levels of the atmosphere set up thereafter which allowed the strong surface high to remain over the area for an extended period of time, essentially blocking off any significant Gulf moisture for the remainder of the month.  By the end of the month, many locations were nearing records for the longest consecutive number of days without measurable rainfall.Measurable rain fell on only one day at many locations throughout the region, with monthly rainfall less than one inch at most reporting stations.  Rainfall deficits exceeding 3 inches were common across most of north Georgia, with rainfall deficits around 2 inches common in the central portion of the state.   Of the four major reporting stations in north Georgia, monthly rainfall totals of only 0.23 inch were observed at Athens,  0.87 at Atlanta, 0.62 at Columbus, and 1.08 at Macon, with deficits of 3.05 inches, 2.18 inches, 1.60 inches, and 1.10 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds ripped the porch off a farmhouse, toppled several trees and power lines, and damaged a pump house, two tractors and an irrigation system.  Reported by the Albany News Herald.

A variety of sources, including the Dublin Courier Herald, local broadcast media, amateur radio operators, and the public reported that an apparent thunderstorm microburst caused extensive wind damage to areas from Dudley to Dublin, especially within the city of Dublin.  The brunt of the damage occurred in the area between U.S. Highway 441 and U.S. Highway 80.  Numerous trees and power lines were reported down throughout the area.  Extensive damage occurred to the Dublin High School athletic facility.  Three of four sides of fencing were damaged on the tennis court, one of the baseball dugouts was destroyed, the roof of the dugout of the softball field was lifted off, and the batting cage was blown away and crumpled up in the nearby woods.  In addition, the vinyl roof was ripped off the school's greenhouse and several small pine trees on the property were blown down as well.  Elsewhere, the roof was ripped off Georgia Power Company's operating headquarters on Pecan Drive.  Within the city, winds blew the aluminum sheeting off the roof of Rando's Fitness in the Westgate Shopping Center and several aluminum business signs were shattered or mangled.
Channel 2 (WSB TV) in Atlanta reported that a child was struck and injured by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds, between 30 and 40 mph at times, caused scattered trees to fall through the day. Winds were generally below advisory criteria, but many rotted trees and limbs fell because of the overall windy and rainy conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wheeler county 911 center reported a few trees down.

The Ben Hill Herald Leader reported that thunderstorms winds, estimated at 50 mph, knocked down numerous trees and power lines around the county, particularly in the Rochelle, Pitts, and Cedar Creek areas. On County Road 24, a large oak tree was blown down knocking several power lines down along with it. The downed power lines sparked several grass fires. The local cooperative observer for the NWS in Abbeville indicated that an awning was blown off the front porch of a mobile home as well.

The Ben Hill Herald-Leader reported that a lightning strike started a fire at a logging operation on Pineview Road which destroyed a fuel trailer with two tanks, an equipment trailer, and a log truck.
The Bleckley county 911 center reported trees down countywide.

The Newnan Times Herald reported that lightning struck and destroyed a vacant mobile home in the Sweetbriar Mobile Home Park at Collinsworth Road and Interstate 85 in extreme northeast Coweta county.  Neighbors reported seeing the lightning strike a pine tree adjacent to the mobile home.  The current traveled from the tree to the mobile home, thus setting it on fire.   No injuries were reported.
The public reported golfball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong Canadian high pressure system settled over the state causing minimum temperatures to drop into the 50s over most of north and central Georgia.   At Ben-Epps Airport in Athens, a new record low of 50 degrees was set which broke the previous record of 54 degrees set in 1976.  Although readings in the 50s were widespread elsewhere, the minimums fell just shy of record levels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators relayed a report of a structural fire caused by lightning. Little information was known about the type or extent of the damage.
The public reported that several trees and power lines were blown down.  There were also reports received of cars being blown off the road by the strong thunderstorm winds.

The Hall county 911 center reported that two trees, blown down by thunderstorm winds, had caused considerable damage to two homes.  One tree fell on the roof of one home causing considerable roof damage.  The other also fell on the roof of a home, but broke several windows out as well.
The Hall county 911 center reported dime size hail.
Large trees down over power lines on U.S. Highway 19.  Reported by a SKYWARN storm spotter.
Several large trees down.  Reported by the Berrien County EMA.
Scattered trees and power lines down.  A few power outages.  Reported by the Tift County EMA.

Numerous downed trees and power lines on U.S. Highway 319 just north of Moultrie.  Traffic detoured around fallen debris.  A few trees down elsewhere throughout the county.  Reported by a SKYWARN storm spotter and the Colquitt County EMA.
Large oak and pine trees down on State Road 33 near the Colquitt-Brooks County line.  Reported by the Colquitt County Sheriff.

The Newton county 911 center reported that several trees were uprooted and that a funnel cloud was observed by the chief of police.  In addition, the public reported that a roof was blown off a gas station.
The public reported dime size hail.  In addition, the top of a 10 inch diameter pine tree was broken off.
The Johnson county 911 center reported trees down countywide.
Large trees and power lines were blown down. The roof of a mobile home was blown off.

The Laurens county 911 center reported roof damage to a house and damage to some utility buildings in Minter. In addition, eight trees were downed on Interstate 16 near Rockledge and other trees were  knocked down near Minter.
The Bibb county Sheriff reported 1.5 inch diameter hail on Rocky Creek Drive at Bloomfield.
The public reported that 3 barns were severely damaged.
The Twiggs county 911 center reported 10 to 15 trees down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll county 911 center reported trees and power lines down along Clem-Lowell road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large Canadian high pressure system settled over the southeastern United States following the cold frontal passage of the 6'th.  This high pressure system brought unseasonably cold air to the state.  Morning low temperatures from the 8'th through the 13'th were 20 to 25 degrees below normal, while high temperatures were 10 to 15 degrees below normal through the period.  Frost was widespread across north and central Georgia, while freezing temperatures were observed in a number of cities.  A number of locations in the northeast Georgia mountains recorded lows in the middle to upper 20s.  Temperatures near 32 were common across north and central Georgia during this period.  Many places reported their first freeze of the fall season a month or more earlier than the seasonal average.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and power lines were blown down.

The Comer News and the Madison county 911 center reported widespread damage across Madison county.  Extensive damage was reported to 3 chicken houses, power lines, and hundreds of trees, some more than 100 years old.  The hardest hit areas of the county were the western sections from Cromers, to Shiloh, to Pocataligo, to Ila.  The Shiloh area was especially hard hit where countless trees were uprooted and snapped in two. In addition, a steeple was toppled at the Jones Chapel in Shiloh by the strong thunderstorm winds.

The Atlanta Journal and Constitution, along with the Clayton county 911 center, ham radio operators, and the public reported strong thunderstorm winds caused widespread destruction of trees across northern Clayton county. The damage path continued from southern Fulton county near College Park through Riverdale to Forest Park and Morrow in Clayton county.  The area from College Park to Riverdale along Georgia Highway 314 bore the brunt of the storm. Hundreds of trees were down on homes and power lines. Parts of Bethsaida Road and Cheryl Lee Road had to be closed because they were blocked by trees.
The public reported 1.00 to 1.50 inch diameter hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon county 911 center reported lots of trees and power lines down across the county.  Damage to a home was incurred in one instance.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens county 911 center reported quarter size hail.
The Laurens county 911 center reported extensive damage to a home. The roof was blown off and the house sustained considerable additional damage.
The Laurens county 911 center reported several trees down.
A few trees down at the intersection of State Road 37 and U.S. Highway 27.  Reported by the Clay County EMA.
Trees and limbs down on Lover's Lane off County Road 526 and U.S. Highway 19, and at Eagle Pond.  Reported by the Lee County EMA.
The Pulaski county 911 center and the public reported trees down and some trees with broken limbs.
Some trees and power lines down.  Reported by the Turner County EMA.
Quarter size hail.  Reported by the Turner County EMA.
The Rockdale Citizen reported that lightning struck a large storage shed in the 2000 block of Georgia Highway 20, just north of Rockdale Drive.
A Jonesboro newspaper reported two homes were struck by lightning.  One home on Camanche Court was set on fire, while only smoke was found at the other house on Lull Water Circle.

The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported that a 64 year-old man was struck by lightning while playing golf at the Cartersville Country Club. He sustained a burn on his foot where the lightning bolt exited his body. He spent the night in the hospital and was released.

The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported lightning struck several homes, setting attic fires in some of them. One home on Forest Hills Drive in Norcross suffered significant damage, one on Sugargate Court near Lilburn sustained minor damage,  and others in Snellville and Centerville suffered little damage.  No injuries were reported.

The De Kalb county 911 center reported lots of trees down on Midway Road, Glenwood Avenue, and Columbia Drive in Avondale Estates; and two small trees down on Memorial Drive and North Hairston Road in Stone Mountain.
The Henry county 911 center reported numerous trees down around McDonough.
The public reported dime size hail from Winston to Fairplay.
Large trees and power lines were blown down.
Many wildfires due to lightning.
Dime size hail.  Reported by a SKYWARN storm spotter.
Some trees and power lines down.  Reported by the Lee County EMA.
Numerous trees down.  Reported by the Irwin County EMA.
The Fayette county 911 center reported dime size hail on Bernhard Road.
The Polk county 911 center and the Rome News Tribune reported trees and power lines down countywide.

The Cartersville Daily News Tribune, the Marietta Daily Journal, and the public reported numerous trees down, some on roads and power lines, throughout the county. Some of the hardest hit areas were Cartersville, Atco, and the Apache Woods subdivision near Lake Allatoona. One tree damaged an outbuilding in Cartersville and another damaged a house.  A woman, traveling on West Avenue in Cartersville during the height of the storm, was lucky to escape injury when a large tree and power line fell on her car.
The Rome News Tribune reported that trees and power lines were down countywide.

The Newnan Times Herald reported tree limbs down throughout Coweta county on roads and power lines.   In one instance a tree limb knocked a power line down onto a minivan on Smokey Road southwest of Newnan  While the car sustained considerable damage, no injuries were reported.

The public reported that an awning and a pole were blown off a house.  The pole below over the roof and hit and damaged a car in the yard.   Further south, a trailer was knocked off its base and several trees were blown down.  Pea size hail was also reported during this same thunderstorm.

The Catoosa county Sheriff's office reported to the Rome News Tribune that numerous trees and powerlines were blown down countywide. Trees fell on power lines,  and blocked roads in many instances. There was also one report of a tree falling on and damaging a vehicle.  Power outages were widespread in the county because of the large number of trees and power lines knocked down by the strong thunderstorm winds.

County 911 dispatchers reported to the Rome News Tribune that trees and power lines were down countywide.  Consequently, nearly the entire county was reported to have been without power during the height of the evening thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down.  Reported by Albany 911.

The Fayette county 911 center and the public reported trees and power lines down along Harp Road south of Fayetteville in the Surrey Park subdivision, where a basketball goal was also reported down.  On Hilo Road, about 3 miles southeast of Fayetteville, several large tree limbs were blown down.   Further south, trees were also down near Starrs Mill.
The public reported some trees down and several shingles blown off the roof of a house.

A storm spotter report was relayed through the Morristown, TN National Weather Service office indicating that roof damage was sustained to the roof of the Piney Grove Baptist Church at the intersection of Burkhalter Gap Road and Lookout Mountain Scenic Highway. The spotter also spotted a funnel cloud in the same general area. In Trenton, an amateur radio operator reported 2 medium size trees down.

The Walker County Messenger, the Rome News Tribune, and ham radio operators reported trees and power lines down countywide.  Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees and limbs. In addition, a vehicle was damaged on Steele Street in Lafayette when a large tree limb fell on it. A billboard was blown down on U.S. Highway 27 in Noble, and a number of other smaller signs were also blown down.

The Rome Daily News, the Marietta Daily Journal, the public, and amateur radio operators reported widespread tree and power line damage countywide. Trees were reported down on homes, a vehicle, and  power lines, and were blocking several roads, especially in Rome.  A house on Lakeview Drive in the Garden Lakes community sustained significant damage when a large oak tree fell on the roof.  The handrail of the St. Peter's Episcopal Church was damaged when a tree fell on the front steps.  A van was also damaged significantly when a large oak tree fell on it during the thunderstorm. There was also a report of damage to the awning and carport of one house in Rome and some windows were blown out of one house about 8 miles south of Rome.
Amateur radio operators reported trees down along Old Ridgeway Road and Morgan Road between Buchanan and Waco.  The public reported trees down on a mobile home in Waco.
The public reported that strong thunderstorm winds picked up a storage shed 10 miles west of Newnan and tossed it about. The storm also knocked down a power line down in Newnan.

The net effect was too little, too late for farmers across North and Central Georgia. As of the end of July, the U.S. Department of Agriculture placed all of the CWA in the range of severe to exceptional drought. The area with the largest departure from normal precipitation during July was a swath from west central Georgia across the state, generally along and south of a line from Atlanta to Athens, and north of a line from Columbus to Macon to Augusta.  No new crop damage or loss estimates were available, but the previous estimates in June seem to represent the entire 2000 growing season. That estimate placed the total for Peachtree City's county warning area at $306.7 million. An increase in precipitation toward the latter part of the month, and forecasts for the continued weakening of La Nina. brought hope that drought conditions that had lasted for over 2 years were beginning to ease. The latest 90 day forecasts called for near normal precipitation over the southeastern U.S.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs county Sheriff reported trees down on Henderson Road.

A variety of sources, including the Georgia Emergency Management Association, an amateur radio operator, local Atlanta TV stations, and the general public, reported downed trees, power lines, and structural damage throughout  Paulding county. Most of the damage was concentrated from Dallas to Hiram near Georgia Highways 6 and 92.  In addition to the numerous reports of downed trees and power lines, 8 buildings sustained minor damage near the intersection of Harris Road and Georgia Highway 6, the siding was blown off a house on Georgia Highway 92 near the Paulding/Cobb county line, and a large pine tree damaged a house chimney near the Carroll county line north of Villa Rica.
The public and an amateur radio operator reported numerous trees down across mainly the eastern half of the county from Villa Rica, to Carrollton, to Whitesburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett county 911 center reported minor street flooding.  In addition a tree was knocked down during the thunderstorm.

The Dawson county Emergency Management Director, amateur radio operators, the Dawson News and Advertiser, and the public all reported extensive amounts of trees and power lines down across the county. The majority of the damage took place along Georgia Highway 53 from the western end of  the county, through Dawsonville, to the eastern end of the county, including Georgia Highway 9. Hundreds of trees, along with power lines, were reported down along Georgia Highway 53.  In addition, The First Baptist Church's Youth Center in Dawsonville was damaged and a car on Georgia Highway  9  was leveled by a large oak tree. Several other homes sustained damage in the county.  A natural gas line was ruptured by a very large uprooted oak tree on West Highway 53. The damage was caused by a supercell thunderstorm that spawned an F0 to weak F1 tornado after moving further east into Hall and Banks counties.

The Dawson News and Advertiser, as well as amateur radio reports and the public, indicated that hail, ranging from the size of dimes to golfballs, fell along Georgia Highway 53 from the western end of the county to the eastern end of the county.
The public reported dime to penny size hail.
The Gwinnett county 911 center reported trees down from Norcross, to Duluth, to Lawrenceville.

The Fayette Citizen reported that a house was struck by lightning on Valley View in the Braelinn Green subdivision.  The house suffered extensive fire and water damage.  No one was home at the time of the incident and no one was injured.
A trained storm spotter reported that a house on Standing Rock Road was struck by lightning.  The extent of the damage was unknown.
The Fayette county 911 center reported street flooding throughout the county.
The Lamar county 911 center and the public reported many trees down across the western half of Lamar county, particularly on Weldon Lake Road and Gordon Road.
The public reported dime size hail near Barnesville.
The Butts county 911 center reported that a tree knocked down by thunderstorm winds caused a power line to fall.

The Dublin Courier Herald reported that within a 2 hour period a nearly stationary thunderstorm knocked trees down on several county roads and power lines in Laurens county.  Lloyd, Hubert, and Brookdale Drive were all blocked by trees.  In addition, a tree fell on a vehicle in Rentz.  The downed power lines, along with numerous cloud-to-ground lightning strikes, knocked the power out to most of the county during the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing surface cyclone off the Carolina coast spread abundant moisture into northeast Georgia, which was still mired ina cold, winter-like temperature regime. The result was another widespread snowfall. The heaviest snow total, around 2 inches, occurred just north of Clayton in northern Rabun County..and in Habersham County near the county seat of Clarkesville. Elsewhere, accumulations ranged from a dusting to around 1 inch.

A very large Canadian high pressure system settled over the southeastern United States following the cold frontal passage of the 6'th.  This high pressure system brought unseasonably cold air to the state.  Morning low temperatures from the 8'th through the 13'th were 20 to 25 degrees below normal, while high temperatures were 10 to 15 degrees below normal through the period.  Frost was widespread across north and central Georgia, while freezing temperatures were observed in a number of cities.  A number of locations in the northeast Georgia mountains recorded lows in the middle to upper 20s.  Temperatures near 32 were common across north and central Georgia during this period.  Many places reported their first freeze of the fall season a month or more earlier than the seasonal average.

After a couple of months of relief from the prolonged dry conditions of the past 2 to 3 years, very dry conditions returned to north and central Georgia during October.  A strong cold front moved through the state on the 6'th, preceded by a line of thunderstorms.  For most locations, this represented the only rain day during the month.  Following the cold front, an unseasonably large and cold dome of high pressure settled over the state.  A stagnant pattern in the upper-levels of the atmosphere set up thereafter which allowed the strong surface high to remain over the area for an extended period of time, essentially blocking off any significant Gulf moisture for the remainder of the month.  By the end of the month, many locations were nearing records for the longest consecutive number of days without measurable rainfall.Measurable rain fell on only one day at many locations throughout the region, with monthly rainfall less than one inch at most reporting stations.  Rainfall deficits exceeding 3 inches were common across most of north Georgia, with rainfall deficits around 2 inches common in the central portion of the state.   Of the four major reporting stations in north Georgia, monthly rainfall totals of only 0.23 inch were observed at Athens,  0.87 at Atlanta, 0.62 at Columbus, and 1.08 at Macon, with deficits of 3.05 inches, 2.18 inches, 1.60 inches, and 1.10 inches respectively.
